paragraphs.text: God appeared to Abram “and said to him: Go out… from thy father’s house, and go into a land that I will show thee. And I will make of thee a great nation” (Gen 12:1–2). Abram left his parents because they would not go with him; but he took Lot with him, because Lot believed the promise that had been given to Abram. And God, though He did not make Lot a sharer in Abram’s inheritance, yet would not allow the sons of Abram either to enter into the inheritance of Lot’s sons. So Abram took Lot and Sarai with him, and went into the land of Canaan (Gen 12:5).

“And there was a famine in the land: and Abram went down into Egypt… And Abram said to Sarai… when the Egyptians shall see thee, they will say: This is his wife: and they will kill me, but they will keep thee alive. Say therefore that thou art his sister, that it may be well with me for thy sake; and my soul shall live because of thee” (Gen 12:10–13). Abram said this as a man; and Sarai was taken into the king’s house, that her love for her husband might be revealed, when even in captivity she did not exchange him for a king, and that the daughters of Sarai might see in her person an instructive example for themselves: as she was not seduced by the kingdom of Egypt, so they should not cling to the idols of Egypt, to garlic and onion; as the whole house of Pharaoh was subjected to plagues for the deliverance of Sarai, so for the deliverance of her children all Egypt would be smitten. Pharaoh’s household were punished for praising Sarai’s beauty and stirring up in the king the desire to take her; Pharaoh himself was punished for taking her by force and compelling her against her will to be his wife. For if Sarai had not feared that they would kill her and her husband, she would not have given herself up to Pharaoh.
